json.html:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
y = df['label']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

model = MultinomialNB()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

def predict_message(msg):
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

sender_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password"

subject = "ML Prediction Result"
body = f"Message: {test_msg}\nPrediction: {result}\nAccuracy: {accuracy}"

msg = MIMEMultipart()
msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
server.starttls()
server.login(sender_email, password)
text = msg.as_string()
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
server.quit()

print("Email sent successfully")
json.text:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
y = df['label']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

model = MultinomialNB()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

def predict_message(msg):
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

sender_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password"

subject = "ML Prediction Result"
body = f"Message: {test_msg}\nPrediction: {result}\nAccuracy: {accuracy}"

msg = MIMEMultipart()
msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
server.starttls()
server.login(sender_email, password)
text = msg.as_string()
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
server.quit()

print("Email sent successfully")

],

vectorizer = CountVectorizer()

json.text:
Here is your code with detailed comments added to explain what each part does:

```python
# Import pandas for data manipulation and analysis
import pandas as pd
# Import train_test_split to divide data into training and testing sets
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Import CountVectorizer to convert text data into numerical vectors (Bag of Words)
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
# Import Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm, commonly used for text classification
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
# Import accuracy_score to evaluate how well the model performed
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
# Import smtplib to handle the Simple Mail Transfer Protocol for sending emails
import smtplib
# Import email modules to structure the email message correctly
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

# Create a sample dictionary containing text messages and their labels (1 for Spam, 0 for Not Spam)
data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

# Convert the dictionary into a Pandas DataFrame for easier processing
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Initialize the vectorizer to convert text into a matrix of token counts
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
# Transform the 'text' column into numerical features (X)
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
# Define the target variable (y) which the model will try to predict
y = df['label']

# Split the data: 80% for training the model and 20% for testing its performance
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# Initialize the Naive Bayes classifier
model = MultinomialNB()
# Train (fit) the model using the training data
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Use the trained model to make predictions on the test data
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
# Calculate the accuracy by comparing predictions to the actual labels
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Define a function to take a new string and predict if it is Spam or Not Spam
def predict_message(msg):
    # Convert the input message into the same numerical format used during training
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    # Predict the class (0 or 1)
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    # Return a readable string based on the prediction result
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

# Test the model with a new custom message
test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

# Email configuration: Replace these with your actual credentials
sender_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password" # Use an App Password if using Gmail, not your main password

# Define the email content
subject = "ML Prediction Result"
body = f"Message: {test_msg}\nPrediction: {result}\nAccuracy: {accuracy}"

# Create a MIMEMultipart object to hold the email headers and body
msg = MIMEMultipart()
msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

# Attach the plain text body to the email
msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

# Connect to the Gmail SMTP server using port 587
server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
# Put the SMTP connection in TLS (Transport Layer Security) mode for security
server.starttls()
# Log in to the email account
server.login(sender_email, password)
# Convert the MIMEMultipart object into a string format
text = msg.as_string()
# Send the email
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
# Terminate the SMTP session
server.quit()

# Print confirmation to the console
print("Email sent successfully")
```

### Important Note for the Email Section:
To make the email code work with Gmail:
1.  **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** must be enabled on your Google account.
2.  You must generate an **"App Password"** from your Google Account settings and use that instead of your regular password.
3.  The code will fail if you use your standard login password due to Google's security protocols.

This script builds a simple **Spam Detection system** using Machine Learning and automatically **emails the results** to a user.

Here is the breakdown of the important details:

1.  **Data Creation:** It starts with a small, manual dataset of text messages labeled as either `1` (Spam) or `0` (Not Spam) using the `pandas` library.
2.  **Text Processing:** Since computers don't understand words, it uses a tool called `CountVectorizer` to convert the text messages into a grid of numbers (word counts).
3.  **Model Training:** 
    *   It splits the data into a **training set** (to teach the model) and a **test set** (to check its accuracy).
    *   It uses the **Multinomial Naive Bayes** algorithm—a classic and effective choice for categorizing text—to learn the difference between spam and normal messages.
4.  **Prediction:** The script provides a specific test message (*"Congratulations! You won a free prize"*) and asks the trained model to predict if it is "Spam" or "Not Spam."
5.  **Email Automation:** Finally, it uses the `smtplib` library to:
    *   Connect to a Gmail server.
    *   Log in using provided credentials.
    *   Send an automated email containing the original message, the model’s prediction, and the model's accuracy score.

**In short:** It teaches a computer to recognize spam and then sends a report of its findings via email.

Here is your code with detailed comments added to explain what each part does:

```python
# Import pandas for data manipulation and analysis
import pandas as pd
# Import train_test_split to divide data into training and testing sets
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Import CountVectorizer to convert text data into numerical vectors (Bag of Words)
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
# Import Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm, commonly used for text classification
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
# Import accuracy_score to evaluate how well the model performed
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
# Import smtplib to handle the Simple Mail Transfer Protocol for sending emails
import smtplib
# Import email modules to structure the email message correctly
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

# Create a sample dictionary containing text messages and their labels (1 for Spam, 0 for Not Spam)
data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

# Convert the dictionary into a Pandas DataFrame for easier processing
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Initialize the vectorizer to convert text into a matrix of token counts
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
# Transform the 'text' column into numerical features (X)
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
# Define the target variable (y) which the model will try to predict
y = df['label']

# Split the data: 80% for training the model and 20% for testing its performance
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# Initialize the Naive Bayes classifier
model = MultinomialNB()
# Train (fit) the model using the training data
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Use the trained model to make predictions on the test data
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
# Calculate the accuracy by comparing predictions to the actual labels
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Define a function to take a new string and predict if it is Spam or Not Spam
def predict_message(msg):
    # Convert the input message into the same numerical format used during training
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    # Predict the class (0 or 1)
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    # Return a readable string based on the prediction result
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

# Test the model with a new custom message
test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

# Email configuration: Replace these with your actual credentials
sender_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password" # Use an App Password if using Gmail, not your main password

# Define the email content
subject = "ML Prediction Result"
body = f"Message: {test_msg}\nPrediction: {result}\nAccuracy: {accuracy}"

# Create a MIMEMultipart object to hold the email headers and body
msg = MIMEMultipart()
msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

# Attach the plain text body to the email
msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

# Connect to the Gmail SMTP server using port 587
server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
# Put the SMTP connection in TLS (Transport Layer Security) mode for security
server.starttls()
# Log in to the email account
server.login(sender_email, password)
# Convert the MIMEMultipart object into a string format
text = msg.as_string()
# Send the email
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
# Terminate the SMTP session
server.quit()

# Print confirmation to the console
print("Email sent successfully")
```

### Important Note for the Email Section:
To make the email code work with Gmail:
1.  **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** must be enabled on your Google account.
2.  You must generate an **"App Password"** from your Google Account settings and use that instead of your regular password.
3.  The code will fail if you use your standard login password due to Google's security protocols.

This script builds a simple **Spam Detection system** using Machine Learning and automatically **emails the results** to a user.

Here is the breakdown of the important details:

1.  **Data Creation:** It starts with a small, manual dataset of text messages labeled as either `1` (Spam) or `0` (Not Spam) using the `pandas` library.
2.  **Text Processing:** Since computers don't understand words, it uses a tool called `CountVectorizer` to convert the text messages into a grid of numbers (word counts).
3.  **Model Training:** 
    *   It splits the data into a **training set** (to teach the model) and a **test set** (to check its accuracy).
    *   It uses the **Multinomial Naive Bayes** algorithm—a classic and effective choice for categorizing text—to learn the difference between spam and normal messages.
4.  **Prediction:** The script provides a specific test message (*"Congratulations! You won a free prize"*) and asks the trained model to predict if it is "Spam" or "Not Spam."
5.  **Email Automation:** Finally, it uses the `smtplib` library to:
    *   Connect to a Gmail server.
    *   Log in using provided credentials.
    *   Send an automated email containing the original message, the model’s prediction, and the model's accuracy score.

**In short:** It teaches a computer to recognize spam and then sends a report of its findings via email.

Here is your code with detailed comments added to explain what each part does:

```python
# Import pandas for data manipulation and analysis
import pandas as pd
# Import train_test_split to divide data into training and testing sets
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Import CountVectorizer to convert text data into numerical vectors (Bag of Words)
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
# Import Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm, commonly used for text classification
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
# Import accuracy_score to evaluate how well the model performed
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
# Import smtplib to handle the Simple Mail Transfer Protocol for sending emails
import smtplib
# Import email modules to structure the email message correctly
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

# Create a sample dictionary containing text messages and their labels (1 for Spam, 0 for Not Spam)
data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

# Convert the dictionary into a Pandas DataFrame for easier processing
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Initialize the vectorizer to convert text into a matrix of token counts
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
# Transform the 'text' column into numerical features (X)
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
# Define the target variable (y) which the model will try to predict
y = df['label']

# Split the data: 80% for training the model and 20% for testing its performance
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# Initialize the Naive Bayes classifier
model = MultinomialNB()
# Train (fit) the model using the training data
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Use the trained model to make predictions on the test data
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
# Calculate the accuracy by comparing predictions to the actual labels
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Define a function to take a new string and predict if it is Spam or Not Spam
def predict_message(msg):
    # Convert the input message into the same numerical format used during training
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    # Predict the class (0 or 1)
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    # Return a readable string based on the prediction result
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

# Test the model with a new custom message
test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

# Email configuration: Replace these with your actual credentials

receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password" # Use an App Password if using Gmail, not your main password

# Create a MIMEMultipart object to hold the email headers and body

msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

# Attach the plain text body to the email
msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

# Connect to the Gmail SMTP server using port 587
server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
# Put the SMTP connection in TLS (Transport Layer Security) mode for security
server.starttls()
# Log in to the email account
server.login(sender_email, password)
# Convert the MIMEMultipart object into a string format
text = msg.as_string()
# Send the email
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
# Terminate the SMTP session
server.quit()

# Print confirmation to the console
print("Email sent successfully")
```

### Important Note for the Email Section:
To make the email code work with Gmail:
1.  **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** must be enabled on your Google account.
2.  You must generate an **"App Password"** from your Google Account settings and use that instead of your regular password.

msg['Subject'] = subject

# Attach the plain text body to the email
msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

# Connect to the Gmail SMTP server using port 587
server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
# Put the SMTP connection in TLS (Transport Layer Security) mode for security
server.starttls()
# Log in to the email account
server.login(sender_email, password)
# Convert the MIMEMultipart object into a string format
text = msg.as_string()
# Send the email
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
# Terminate the SMTP session
server.quit()

# Print confirmation to the console
print("Email sent successfully")
```

### Important Note for the Email Section:
To make the email code work with Gmail:
1.  **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** must be enabled on your Google account.
2.  You must generate an **"App Password"** from your Google Account settings and use that instead of your regular password.
3.  The code will fail if you use your standard login password due to Google's security protocols.

code with summary :

This script builds a simple **Spam Detection system** using Machine Learning and automatically **emails the results** to a user.

Here is the breakdown of the important details:

1.  **Data Creation:** It starts with a small, manual dataset of text messages labeled as either `1` (Spam) or `0` (Not Spam) using the `pandas` library.
2.  **Text Processing:** Since computers don't understand words, it uses a tool called `CountVectorizer` to convert the text messages into a grid of numbers (word counts).
3.  **Model Training:** 
    *   It splits the data into a **training set** (to teach the model) and a **test set** (to check its accuracy).
    *   It uses the **Multinomial Naive Bayes** algorithm—a classic and effective choice for categorizing text—to learn the difference between spam and normal messages.
4.  **Prediction:** The script provides a specific test message (*"Congratulations! You won a free prize"*) and asks the trained model to predict if it is "Spam" or "Not Spam."
5.  **Email Automation:** Finally, it uses the `smtplib` library to:
    *   Connect to a Gmail server.
    *   Log in using provided credentials.
    *   Send an automated email containing the original message, the model’s prediction, and the model's accuracy score.

**In short:** It teaches a computer to recognize spam and then sends a report of its findings via email.

This message has been converted into a professional HTML format suitable for email. It includes syntax highlighting for the Python code and structured sections for readability. ```html ML Code and Summary

Code with Comments

Here is your code with detailed comments added to explain what each part does:

# Import pandas for data manipulation and analysis
import pandas as pd
# Import train_test_split to divide data into training and testing sets
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Import CountVectorizer to convert text data into numerical vectors (Bag of Words)
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
# Import Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm, commonly used for text classification
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
# Import accuracy_score to evaluate how well the model performed
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
# Import smtplib to handle the Simple Mail Transfer Protocol for sending emails
import smtplib
# Import email modules to structure the email message correctly
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart

# Create a sample dictionary containing text messages and their labels (1 for Spam, 0 for Not Spam)
data = {
    'text': [
        "Win money now", "Hello friend how are you",
        "Free lottery win", "Important meeting tomorrow",
        "Claim your prize now", "Let's catch up soon"
    ],
    'label': [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
}

# Convert the dictionary into a Pandas DataFrame for easier processing
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Initialize the vectorizer to convert text into a matrix of token counts
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
# Transform the 'text' column into numerical features (X)
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(df['text'])
# Define the target variable (y) which the model will try to predict
y = df['label']

# Split the data: 80% for training the model and 20% for testing its performance
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# Initialize the Naive Bayes classifier
model = MultinomialNB()
# Train (fit) the model using the training data
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Use the trained model to make predictions on the test data
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
# Calculate the accuracy by comparing predictions to the actual labels
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Define a function to take a new string and predict if it is Spam or Not Spam
def predict_message(msg):
    # Convert the input message into the same numerical format used during training
    msg_vector = vectorizer.transform([msg])
    # Predict the class (0 or 1)
    prediction = model.predict(msg_vector)
    # Return a readable string based on the prediction result
    return "Spam" if prediction[0] == 1 else "Not Spam"

# Test the model with a new custom message
test_msg = "Congratulations! You won a free prize"
result = predict_message(test_msg)

# Email configuration
sender_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
receiver_email = "your_email@gmail.com"
password = "your_app_password" 

# Define the email content
subject = "ML Prediction Result"
body = f"Message: {test_msg}\nPrediction: {result}\nAccuracy: {accuracy}"

# Create a MIMEMultipart object
msg = MIMEMultipart()
msg['From'] = sender_email
msg['To'] = receiver_email
msg['Subject'] = subject

msg.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain'))

# SMTP Connection
server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
server.starttls()
server.login(sender_email, password)
text = msg.as_string()
server.sendmail(sender_email, receiver_email, text)
server.quit()

print("Email sent successfully")
⚠️ Important Note for the Email Section:

To make the email code work with Gmail:

  1. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) must be enabled on your Google account.
  2. You must generate an "App Password" from your Google Account settings and use that instead of your regular password.
  3. The code will fail if you use your standard login password due to Google's security protocols.

Code Summary

This script builds a simple Spam Detection system using Machine Learning and automatically emails the results to a user.

  • Data Creation: Starts with a small dataset of text messages labeled as 1 (Spam) or 0 (Not Spam) using pandas.
  • Text Processing: Uses CountVectorizer to convert words into numerical data that the computer can process.
  • Model Training: Utilizes the Multinomial Naive Bayes algorithm to learn differences between spam and valid messages.
  • Prediction: Tests the model with a custom string and categorizes it accordingly.
  • Email Automation: Uses smtplib to log into a Gmail server and send a report including the prediction and accuracy score.
